channel.title: AI Home Building
channel.description: Research, analysis, and reporting on how artificial intelligence is transforming home design, building, and project management.

item.title: The AI Drew Your Floor Plan in 30 Seconds. Your Building Department Will Reject It in 30 Minutes.
item.description: AI floor plan generators can produce residential layouts in seconds. A code-by-code audit against IRC 2021 reveals they cover just 7% of what a plan reviewer will check, and no tool on the market closes the gap between a generated layout and a buildable design.
item.author: Elena Vasquez

item.title: Your Insurer’s Algorithm Valued Your $500K Home at $350K. The 12-State Audit That Could Change the Math.
item.description: Insurers deploy billion-dollar AI systems to estimate rebuild costs while homeowners have almost nothing to fight back with. A Harvard study of 100 million mortgages found the average homeowner covers just 50% of actual rebuild cost.
item.author: Catherine Chen

item.title: Your Panel Has 200 Amps. Your All-Electric Home Needs 280. A $3,500 Smart Panel Says It Can Close the Gap.
item.description: Home electrification is blowing past the 200-amp residential standard. Smart panels from Span, ABB, and Schneider promise AI load management can dodge a $15,000-$30,000 service upgrade. We ran the break-even numbers and found where physics wins.
item.author: Jake Kowalski

item.title: Jensen Huang Designed a House on Stage. Your Architect Should Be Relieved, Not Worried.
item.description: NVIDIA's RTX Spark demo compressed weeks of schematic design into minutes. But the gap between a beautiful render and a buildable set of construction documents is exactly where architects earn their fee.
item.author: Elena Vasquez

item.title: Your Electrician Made $20 an Hour. A Data Center Offered $35. He Didn't Come Back.
item.description: Data center construction spending hit $50.7 billion this year. Meta just pledged $115 million to train workers for those sites. Meanwhile, a Texas homebuilder lost five electricians and added two months to every project.
item.author: Marcus Washington

item.title: A $15,000 Retrofit Cuts Your Wildfire Risk by 31 Percent. California Finally Made That Number Count.
item.description: California's new catastrophe modeling rules force insurers to price home hardening features into wildfire premiums. A CDI-NAIC study quantifies the payoff: 31-35% reduction in average annual loss. Here's the retrofit math for a 2,000-square-foot home.
item.author: Priya Greenwood

item.title: Your City’s Permit Office Took Six Months to Review a Set of Residential Plans. An AI Did It in Seven Days.
item.description: Cities and builders are deploying AI plan-review tools on both sides of the permit counter simultaneously. Honolulu cut residential permit backlogs from six months to one week. Austin cut staff review time in half. What happens when both sides of the bottleneck get squeezed at once.
item.author: Frank DeLuca

item.title: Your Air Conditioner Is Almost Certainly Too Big for Your House. The Building Code That Should Have Prevented It Has Been Ignored for 17 Years.
item.description: 91% of residential HVAC systems are oversized. Building codes have required precise load calculations since 2009. AI tools can automate them in 15 minutes. But the real barrier isn't technology — it's that your contractor makes more money selling you the bigger unit.
item.author: Priya Greenwood

item.title: An AI Agent Designed a House on Stage in Taipei. Your City Still Wants a Human Signature.
item.description: AI floor plan tools cost $20/month. Professional plan review and stamping costs $5,000-$15,000. Nobody's figured out who pays when the AI gets the flashing detail wrong.
item.author: Elena Vasquez

item.title: The Median Home Appraiser Is 60 Years Old. The Industry's Plan Isn't to Train His Replacement.
item.description: With 80% of licensed appraisers over 50, the industry isn't recruiting new talent. It's building AI to eliminate the job entirely. Veros, ATTOM, and Fannie Mae are already writing the playbook.
item.author: Marcus Washington

item.title: Construction Costs Rose 6.2% in Four Months. Your Builder's Estimate Didn't Move.
item.description: Tariffs, war inflation, and material price volatility have made traditional construction estimates obsolete within weeks. AI pricing tools promise real-time tracking, but the builders who need them most can't afford them.
item.author: Frank DeLuca

item.title: An AI Looked at Your Roof From Space. It Decided You're Uninsurable.
item.description: ZestyAI, Verisk, and CoreLogic now score individual homes on 200+ data points from satellite imagery. Two neighbors can get opposite insurance decisions. Unlike FICO, you can't check your score.
item.author: Priya Greenwood

item.title: In California, Your Barber Needs 1,000 Hours of Training. Your Home Inspector Needs Zero.
item.description: 14 states don't require home inspectors to be licensed. AI tools are arriving that could standardize quality or give unqualified inspectors a professional veneer. We mapped the regulatory gap, followed the money, and found that the cost of missed defects dwarfs the entire inspection industry.
item.author: Catherine Chen

item.title: Copper Hit $6.65 a Pound. Your New Home Needs 439 Pounds of It.
item.description: COMEX copper set an all-time record on June 2. Section 232 tariffs layer 25-50% duties on top. We ran the math on what that adds to a 2,100 sq ft house, scored PEX and aluminum as substitutions, and explained why a tariff on 16% of imports raises costs across 100% of the market.
item.author: Jake Kowalski

item.title: Utah Gave an AI a Law License. It Charges $117 to Review the Contracts Your GC Never Reads.
item.description: Superlegal launched the first AI law firm authorized to practice law in the United States, reviewing construction contracts for $117 each under Utah's regulatory sandbox. The math changes everything for small contractors. The liability questions change it back.
item.author: Catherine Chen

item.title: You Probably Overpaid Your Property Taxes Last Year. The County Is Counting on You Not Checking.
item.description: 40% of U.S. homes may be overassessed. Only 3-5% of owners appeal. AI platforms are automating the protest process with 86% success rates, but the real story is who benefits and who gets left behind.
item.author: Frank DeLuca

item.title: AI Checked Your Handrail Height in Seconds. It Can't Tell You If Your ADU Is Legal.
item.description: AI plan review tools are slashing permit turnaround from 30 days to 2. But they work on prescriptive building codes, not discretionary zoning. The Urban Institute tested AI against Minneapolis's 467-page zoning code. The results were sobering.
item.author: Catherine Chen

item.title: The Tile Robot Laid 100,000 Square Meters of Floor. None of Them Had a Toilet in the Way.
item.description: Tile-laying robots claim 5-6x speed gains and a $1.96 billion market. The catch: residential tile work is mostly bathrooms, showers, and backsplashes. The robot handles the easy 40%. A human still does the hard 60%.
item.author: Jake Kowalski

item.title: Homebuyer Trust in AI Fell 14 Points in One Year. The Staging Fakes Did That.
item.description: Cotality's 2026 survey shows trust in AI to help find a home dropped from 30% to 16%. AI staging fraud poisoned the well for tools that could actually help buyers. Two states require disclosure. Forty-eight do not.
item.author: Catherine Chen

item.title: The AI Drew 31 Floor Plans. Only 8 Had Windows That Worked.
item.description: AI floor plan generators claim to handle 70-75% of schematic design. Peer-reviewed daylight simulations across five climate zones reveal they can't orient a window to catch the sun. The gap between room count and livability is measurable.
item.author: Elena Vasquez

item.title: Your Builder Waited 7 Days for a Draw. The AI Approved It in 3 Minutes. The Bank Found More Problems, Not Fewer.
item.description: Built Technologies' Draw Agent processes construction loan draws in 3 minutes that took humans 20 to 40 hours, catching 4x more compliance risks while cutting turn time 60%. For builders bleeding cash between draws, AI just fixed the financial plumbing.
item.author: Frank DeLuca

item.title: Katerra Burned $2.2 Billion on a Megafactory. Reframe Systems Spends 100 Days on a Microfactory. The Math Finally Works.
item.description: A new generation of AI-powered microfactories claims to crack factory-built housing where Katerra, Veev, and Skender failed. The structural difference: 100-day standup, Amazon logistics DNA, and revenue before scale.
item.author: Jake Kowalski

item.title: Your Permit Application Has a 63% Chance of Bouncing. Denver Is Paying $4.6 Million to Fix That.
item.description: Cities are deploying AI pre-check tools that catch missing documents and code errors before formal permit review. Denver went from 37% first-try approval to targeting 80%. The real question: should you wait for your city to deploy one, or bring your own?
item.author: Frank DeLuca

item.title: An AI Scored Your Roof From a Satellite. Your Insurer Never Knocked.
item.description: Computer vision models are grading 38% of American roofs as moderate-to-poor condition from aerial imagery alone. The scores determine your premium, your renewability, and increasingly whether you get covered at all.
item.author: Catherine Chen

item.title: Your Contractor Waited 7 Days to Strip Forms. The Concrete Was Ready in 3.
item.description: A $50 wireless sensor embedded in your foundation pour calculates real-time concrete strength using ASTM C1074 maturity curves. It replaces guesswork with data and saves 3-10 days of schedule.
item.author: Jake Kowalski

item.title: Your Builder Waited 11 Days for a Draw. The AI Approved the Next One in 9 Minutes.
item.description: The construction industry loses $273 billion a year to slow payments. AI draw agents are compressing week-long reviews into minutes. For the residential builder juggling three projects on one checking account, this matters more than any robot on the job site.
item.author: Frank DeLuca

item.title: AI Inspection Tools Write Faster Reports. They Don’t See Through Walls.
item.description: Every major AI home inspection startup is optimizing report generation, not defect detection. The ASHI Standards of Practice already prohibit inspectors from opening walls. AI processing visible-light photos of those same walls doesn’t change what’s hidden behind them.
item.author: Catherine Chen

item.title: Your AI Kitchen Render Forgot About the Plumbing. The Contractor Didn't.
item.description: AI renovation visualization tools generate photorealistic rooms with zero structural knowledge. 70% of homeowners already go over budget. When the render becomes the brief, the gap between beautiful and buildable gets expensive fast.
item.author: Elena Vasquez

item.title: The No. 2 Lumber in Your Walls Might Not Be No. 2. AI Grading Is Fixing That.
item.description: Human lumber graders overestimate value by 20%, according to the USDA Forest Service. New AI vision systems at sawmills are 31% more accurate. For builders buying framing packages, the downstream math on callbacks and rework changes substantially.
item.author: Frank DeLuca

item.title: Your Insurer's AI Already Scored Your Home for Wildfire Risk. You Weren't Told.
item.description: ZestyAI found 1.2 million California homes rated HIGH risk by AI but LOW risk by FEMA. The construction features that move your score cost $2,800 to fix. The math on when that pays for itself is simpler than you think.
item.author: Jake Kowalski

item.title: Your City's AI Approved the Plans. The Foundation Cracked. Who Pays?
item.description: AI plan review cuts permitting from weeks to 30 minutes across 26 states. But when the algorithm misses a code violation, the liability chain collapses: vendors disclaim, municipalities claim immunity, architects point to the approval stamp. Homeowners are left holding the bill.
item.author: Catherine Chen

item.title: Steel Tariffs Are Accidentally Decarbonizing Buildings. Lumber Tariffs Are Undoing It.
item.description: 50% steel tariffs push builders toward mass timber, cutting embodied carbon by 40%. But 35% lumber tariffs push them back. AI carbon-tracking tools could navigate this contradiction, but almost nobody uses them.
item.author: Priya Greenwood

item.title: The Industry Spent a Decade Teaching AI to Fly Drones and Drive Excavators. Nobody Taught It to Answer an RFI.
item.description: Construction professionals lose 14 hours a week to paperwork. AI research spends less than 1% of its attention on the problem. The $177 billion disconnect between where the money goes and where the time goes.
item.author: Frank DeLuca

item.title: You Installed a $500 Smart Water Valve for the Insurance Discount. The Fine Print Says Keep It Plugged In or Lose Coverage.
item.description: Insurance carriers are offering 5-10% premium discounts for smart water shutoff valves. What the brochure skips: protective device endorsements that make the discount a contractual obligation, not a reward.
item.author: Catherine Chen

item.title: The Construction Industry Lost 1.4 Million Tradespeople in 18 Years. It Gained 800,000 Managers.
item.description: Microsoft and OpenAI are pouring money into AI training for construction workers. NABTU's programs reach 1,500 instructors. The industry has 7.1 million tradespeople. The math does not work, and the structural shift underneath it is worse than the training gap.
item.author: Marcus Washington

item.title: Your Energy Model Promised 25% Savings. Across 140,000 Homes, the Average Was 7%.
item.description: A systematic review of 140,977 retrofitted households found average energy savings of 7.2%, not the 20-30% that models predict. AI energy tools add decimal places to the same wrong number.
item.author: Priya Greenwood

item.title: Forty Cities Use AI to Review Building Permits. Nineteen Thousand Don't. The Math Isn't the Problem.
item.description: AI plan review cuts permit times by 50-70% where deployed. Fewer than 40 of America's 20,000 permit-issuing jurisdictions use it. The gap costs homebuyers an estimated $2.4 billion a year in avoidable delays.
item.author: Catherine Chen

item.title: Your AI Scheduler Shaved Three Weeks Off Your Build. The Inspector Queue Added Four Back.
item.description: AI scheduling tools promise 20% faster construction timelines. But when 33-44% of your build calendar is government wait time, that optimization hits a hard ceiling. We ran the numbers.
item.author: Frank DeLuca

item.title: Your AI Floor Plan Has No Ceiling. Literally.
item.description: Zero consumer AI floor plan generators model ceiling height as a design variable. The vertical dimension — the thing that makes a room feel like a cathedral or a cave — is invisible to every tool homeowners are using to design their homes.
item.author: Elena Vasquez

item.title: ICON's $899,000 Titan Prints Your Walls for $20 a Square Foot. The Other 80% of Your House Still Costs the Same.
item.description: ICON launched the Titan Print System at $899K. The $20/sq ft wall claim is real, but walls are 20% of a home's cost. We ran the amortization math for builders at every scale.
item.author: Jake Kowalski

item.title: Your Green Home Scored a HERS 40. Its Embodied Carbon Is Identical to the Code-Minimum House Next Door.
item.description: A 100-home Massachusetts study found above-code homes are no better at limiting embodied carbon than standard builds. Your energy model optimizes half the problem.
item.author: Priya Greenwood

item.title: Your Builder Uses AI. Your Contract Doesn't Mention It. Guess Who Loses That Argument.
item.description: 49% of homebuilders now use AI tools. Zero standard construction contracts address what happens when those tools fail. The legal gap is real, and it's your problem.
item.author: Catherine Chen

item.title: Your AI Kitchen Layout Passes the Eye Test. It Fails 27 of 31 Industry Design Rules.
item.description: AI kitchen design tools generate stunning layouts that violate the NKBA's 31 planning guidelines. Here's what they miss and what it costs you when the cabinets arrive.
item.author: Elena Vasquez

item.title: Your Builder Manages a $600K Project With the Same Software That Runs Your Fantasy Football League
item.description: Procore's new AI agents review submittals, file RFIs, and draft daily logs autonomously. They cost $21K a year. Most custom home builders can't break even on that until $2M in annual volume. The residential AI gap is now a canyon.
item.author: Frank DeLuca
